1:
The chapter opens with a single terse notice — Moab rebelled against Israel after Ahab's death — and the witnesses spread it across history, providence, and morality. The grammatical-historical commentators fill in the political frame. Ellicott traces the vassalage back to David, who reduced Moab (2 Sam. 8:2), and then reaches beyond Scripture to the famous Moabite Stone, on which Mesha, 'son of Chemosh-gad, king of Moab,' records how Omri had oppressed his land and how he at last 'saw his desire' upon Ahab's house — a pagan monument that quietly corroborates the sacred history. Keil and Delitzsch set the verse within the whole sweep of the books of the Kings, the theocracy told through its monarchs, and note that Ahaziah, already stricken, could do nothing to hold the tributary down. Both observe that the revolt is dropped in here parenthetically and taken up again in the war of 2 Kings 3:4-27.

The reformed Gill keeps to the occasion: Moab had paid tribute since David's day, and now, emboldened by Ahab's ill success against Syria and by his death, refused the yoke, gambling on the weakness of his successor. The wesleyan-arminian voices add the shape of the divided kingdom. Clarke and Wesley note that after Solomon's realm split, Moab fell to Israel and Edom to Judah, each bound to the kingdom it bordered; when both crowns grew weak and were forsaken by God, the subject peoples seized their moment — Moab now, Edom a little later — and Jehoram would afterward league with Judah and Edom to reduce them (2 Kings 3:5).

It is the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry who presses the moral logic the bare annal conceals. He reads Ahaziah as a wicked king already under God's rebukes, and sets the two rebellions side by side: 'when he rebelled against God, and revolted from his allegiance to him, Moab rebelled against Israel.' Man's revolt from God, Henry observes, is often punished by the rebellion of those who owe subjection to him; and he turns it inward — 'if men break their covenants with us, and neglect their duty, we must reflect upon our breach of covenant with God.' Sin, he concludes in his plain way, 'weakens and impoverishes us.'

The voices do not really contend here. The historians establish that Moab's independence, lost under David and Omri and held by Ahab, revived the instant Israel's king fell sick and unsteady; the divines read in that timing the hand of a God who lets a rebel throne feel the rebellion of its own vassals. The chapter's opening loss is thus already its first sermon: the kingdom that forsook the Lord finds its own borders forsaking it.
2:
The witnesses gather around two objects here — the lattice through which Ahaziah fell and the fly-god to whom he turned — and the second draws out a real dispute. On the accident the grammatical-historical commentators are content with the architecture: Ellicott takes the word for 'through' to mean that the king was leaning out over the sill of the latticed window in his roof-chamber and pitched into the court or a gallery below, several storeys down, badly hurt but not killed; Keil and Delitzsch agree it was a latticed window or door, and dismiss the rabbinic guess of a skylight in the floor. Gill canvasses the same options — a network window, a balcony rail that gave way, a roof-light trodden through — and Wesley reads it 'through the lattice in the battlements of the roof.'

The graver question is the god. All agree the name Baal-zebub means 'lord of the fly,' the tutelary deity of Ekron — the northernmost Philistine city, Ellicott notes, and so the nearest to Samaria. But why 'lord of flies'? The wesleyan-arminian reading, given by Wesley and Clarke, takes it protectively: an idol so called because he was thought to deliver the country from swarms of flies, as the Greeks named a fly-averting Zeus and Hercules; Clarke adds that he began as a kind of charm against insects and grew into 'a very respectable devil.' Against this the grammatical-historical voices press a darker sense. Ellicott, granting the fly-averting parallel, judges the true significance to lie in myiomancy — the ancient Babylonian divination that read omens from the movements of flies — so that the god of flies was the god one consulted for oracles; Keil and Delitzsch likewise make him no mere insect-queller but a fly-shaped deity, the Myia-god of the Greek translators. Both note the later Jewish spelling Beelzeboul, which puns on the word for dung.

Beneath the philology the divines hear the theology. Clarke recalls that in the New Testament Beelzebub becomes a name for the prince of the devils himself (Matt. 12:24), so that Ahaziah, spurning the God at his own door, sends across the border to inquire of Satan under another name. Wesley concedes the hard fact that such idol-oracles did, by God's permission, sometimes return an answer, 'though generally dark and doubtful' — which only sharpens the folly of preferring them to the plain word of the living God. The scene is set: a king who has just learned from a broken lattice that there is but a step between him and death asks a dumb idol whether he will live, and never thinks to ask the God who alone appoints the step.
3:
With the messengers already on the road to Ekron, God intercepts them through his prophet, and the witnesses read the interception as the God of Israel refusing to be ignored. The grammatical-historical commentators handle the mechanics and the wording. Ellicott corrects the tense — 'Now the angel had said' — and defends the definite article: it is 'the angel of the Lord,' the same messenger met elsewhere (compare 2 Kings 19:35), who sends Elijah up to meet the envoys at the gate of hill-set Samaria. He catches, too, the pointed phrase 'the king of Samaria' rather than 'of Israel' — a stroke of Judaean feeling that will not grant the apostate the dignity of the covenant name. Keil and Delitzsch identify the speaker as the covenant Angel, mediator of the invisible God's revelations, and gloss the stinging question with its parallel idiom (Exodus 14:11): is it 'for want of a God in Israel' that the king sends abroad?

The reformed Gill keeps the identification humbler — 'one of the ministering spirits sent by the Lord' — but hears the same rebuke.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley draws out the insult the question implies: to send to Baal-zebub is to cast contempt on the God of Israel, 'as if he were either ignorant of the event of thy disease, or unable to give thee relief,' as if the fly-god of Ekron had more skill and power than the Maker of Israel. Ellicott points the contrast further with the prophet Micah's boast that the nations may walk each in the name of its god, 'but we will walk in the name of Jehovah our God for ever' (Micah 4:5) — precisely the confession Ahaziah has thrown away.

The witnesses do not divide over the substance. Whether the messenger be a common angel or the Angel of the covenant, the errand is one: the living God plants his prophet squarely across the path of an idolatrous embassy, so that the answer the king sought from a dumb oracle comes back to him, unbidden and unbearable, from the mouth of the God he had slighted. The very haste of the interception says what the chapter means to say — that there is a God in Israel, and that he is neither ignorant of the king's disease nor indifferent to the king's contempt.
4:
This is the sentence itself, and though the material is spare the witnesses let its two words — 'surely die' — carry the whole weight of the chapter's argument. The grammatical-historical commentators mark its function. Ellicott reads it as the divine answer to the king's faithlessness and, still more, as the proof God means to give 'by the event that there is a God in Israel, whose oracle is unerring,' setting the true God's sure word against the flies of Ekron much as Elijah once set him against the prophets of Baal (compare 1 Kings 18:24). He suspects the compiler has abridged the account, the doom falling almost abruptly after 'thus saith the Lord.' Keil and Delitzsch simply let the oracle stand and record that 'Elijah departed' to carry it out.

The reformed Gill fixes attention on the speaker — 'Jehovah, the only true God' — so that the contrast is not between two oracles of comparable standing but between the One who is and the idol that is not.

The wesleyan-arminian Clarke states the theology most bluntly: this word comes from 'the true God, he in whose hands are both life and death, who can kill and make alive; Baal-zebub can do nothing.' God has determined that the king shall die, and no fly-god can reverse it.

The witnesses stand together. The verse is short because it needs to be: where the king asked whether he would recover, the God he bypassed answers not with a probability but with a certainty, and the certainty is death. The tribute of Moab was the first thing to slip from Ahaziah's failing grip; his own life is the second; and the reason for both, the divines agree, is one — a man who will not seek the living God is left with nothing that can be relied upon, not his kingdom, not his health, not the dumb god he preferred to his Maker. The unerring word that Ellicott presses is the whole point of the miracle-history that follows: heaven will vindicate by fire a sentence that Ekron could never have spoken. Gill's stress on the speaker and Clarke's on his power meet in one confession — the God who alone is has spoken, and what he speaks concerning life and death is done.
5:
The messengers, sent to Ekron, return within the hour, and the witnesses read their premature homecoming as the first tremor of the oracle's authority. The grammatical-historical commentators supply the detail. Keil and Delitzsch note that the envoys did not recognise Elijah, yet turned back at once and reported; and they weigh the king's soon-to-be-asked question about the man's 'manner' (mishpat), the word for the distinguishing peculiarity of a person, his figura et habitus. Ellicott reads 'they returned unto him' as returned to Ahaziah, and lays the stress where the Hebrew does — on the king's astonished 'Why? — why have ye returned?' A menacing stranger who knew their errand before they spoke it would strike even pagan couriers as a divine warning too perilous to disregard, and so they came back without ever reaching the shrine. Though Elijah was wholly unknown to them by name, Ellicott adds, such an interposition could not be mistaken for chance.

The reformed Gill accounts for their instinct: from the man's habit, his gravity, and the authority with which he spoke — and above all from his uncanny knowledge of their secret commission — they concluded he was a prophet of the Lord, and dared not go on. The very boldness of the interception, breaking in upon a royal errand on the open road, was itself a credential.

The witnesses agree, and the picture they compose is quietly devastating. The king had reached past the God of Israel to a Philistine oracle; the God of Israel reaches back, plants one hairy prophet on a hillside, and the whole embassy wheels around and comes home with its question unasked and its answer already delivered. There was no need for the messengers to consult Baal-zebub about whether their master would recover; the living God had told them, and their sudden return is itself the sign that the word they carry is not to be trifled with. What the king meant to learn by stealth from Ekron he must now hear, aloud and unavoidable, from his own servants' mouths — and their fear, written in the very speed of their return, is the first witness that the word is true.
6:
Called to account, the messengers repeat verbatim the word that turned them home, and the witnesses attend to the faithfulness of the report and the force of its verbs. The grammatical-historical commentators fix the grammar. Ellicott notes that Elijah had told the couriers 'ye are going' to inquire of Baal-zebub, and that they now relay it to the king as 'thou sendest' — art sending — laying the guilt exactly where it belongs, on the master who dispatched them. He is careful to clear the servants of any craft: they were not, as one interpreter supposed, shifting the blame from themselves to their lord, for they had no choice but to obey the royal command; they simply hand back the oracle unaltered. Keil and Delitzsch print the whole speech again and mark that this is now the second of three verbatim deliveries of the same sentence.

The reformed Gill supplies the connective tissue — 'there came a man up to meet us' — and lets the plainness of the report stand, the servants adding neither comment nor apology.

What strikes the witnesses is the terrible economy of the thing. The king's question was, 'Shall I recover?' The answer, carried back by his own frightened envoys, is the exact reverse of what he hoped: because he sent to Ekron as though there were no God in Israel, he shall not come down from the bed he has gone up to, but shall surely die. The messengers add nothing and soften nothing; and in that unembellished repetition the divines hear the character of a true word from God — it does not bend to the dignity of the hearer, and it loses none of its edge in the mouths of those who merely pass it on. That the same words fall three times in this short chapter — to the couriers, from the couriers, and soon to the king's face — is itself a kind of sentence: the verdict will not be revised. Ahaziah wanted a private forecast; he is given a public judgement, and it is already circulating through his own household.
7:
The king, unsettled by the report, presses for a description — 'what manner of man was he?' — and the witnesses treat the question as the hinge on which recognition turns. The grammatical-historical commentators dwell on the key word. Ellicott explains that mishpat here does not mean judgement but the external characteristics and visible peculiarities by which one man is distinguished from his fellows — 'sort,' 'fashion,' 'style,' as the Vulgate renders it, 'of what figure and habit is that man?' It is a request for a portrait, not a name. Keil and Delitzsch make the same point and draw from it a small theology of appearance: the servants describe the prophet 'according to his outward appearance, which in a man of character is a reflection of his inner man.'

The reformed Gill keeps it simple — the king asks after the man's fashion, his habit, his clothing — and so prepares for the single decisive detail of the next verse. He does not yet name his fear, but the question betrays it: only one man in Israel would have dared to meet a royal embassy on the road with a sentence of death.

There is no dispute to be had over so plain a question, and the witnesses use it chiefly to slow the narrative at its turning-point. Ahaziah does not yet know whom he has provoked; his own couriers hold the answer in the memory of a garment and a girdle; and the moment they sketch the figure, the king will name his old adversary without another word. The verse is a held breath — the interval between an oracle received from a stranger and the dreadful recognition of who the stranger was — and the commentators are content to let it do that quiet, suspenseful work, knowing that the description to come will fall on the king like the return of a familiar judgment he had hoped never to face again. The very form of his question, they observe, already half-suspects the answer; and so they let the description hang unspoken for a beat, that the reader, like the king, may feel the weight of the recognition before the name of Elijah is ever pronounced.
8:
From a single sentence of description the king reads the whole verdict, and the witnesses divide amiably over just what the description means. The grammatical-historical commentators weigh the Hebrew 'lord of hair.' Ellicott argues it points not to the man's body but to his dress — a hairy mantle, the recognised badge of the prophetic office from Elijah onward — and marshals the parallels: the 'rough garment' the false prophets would later wear to counterfeit the order (Zechariah 13:4), and John the Baptist, the second Elijah, 'clad in camel's hair' with a leather girdle (Matt. 3:4). The girdle clinches it: a strap of mere leather, where the common girdle was of linen and often costly, marked the poorest of men, and so proclaimed the prophet's studied contempt for earthly display and his mourning over the nation's sin (compare Isaiah 20:2). Keil and Delitzsch agree the phrase means a man wrapped in a rough skin garment, not merely one shaggy of body.

The wesleyan-arminian Clarke lays out both readings without forcing a choice: 'he was a hairy man' may mean that he wore a rough coat of camel's hair or a dressed skin with the hair left on, or it may mean that his own hair and beard hung long and neglected; either way he stood in the line of the old prophets, who 'wandered about in sheepskins and goatskins' (Heb. 11:37). Wesley leans to the garment, noting that such rough hairy clothing was the proper habit of the prophets, worn also by John the Baptist so that 'by his very outward habit he might represent Elijah, in whose spirit and power he came.' Gill allows the same latitude — long unshorn hair and beard, or a mantle of goat's or camel's hair like the Baptist's.

The debate, then, is narrow and friendly: whether the mark of Elijah was the wildness of his own hair or the roughness of his cloak, with Ellicott and Wesley firm for the mantle and Clarke and the Greek translators leaving room for the hair. On the thing that matters they are wholly one. 'It is Elijah the Tishbite' — the king needs no name spoken, for the garment is the man, and the man is the standing rebuke of his father's house. That the New Testament dresses John the Baptist in the very same austerity is, for these witnesses, no accident: the prophet who confronts a court's sin comes stripped of the court's finery, carrying in his leather and hair the sermon he has come to preach.
9:
Now the narrative turns violent, and the witnesses fasten on the collision between a king's warrant and a prophet's God. The grammatical-historical commentators establish the setting and the menace. Ellicott explains the military unit — Israel's army was ordered by thousands, hundreds, and fifties, each with its captain (compare Numbers 31:14; 1 Samuel 8:12) — and reads plain hostility in the sending of armed men. He hears irony in the summons: the captain's 'the king hath said, Come down' sets the king's word, emphatically, against the man of God, as if royal power were irresistible even to Heaven's messenger; and the very title 'thou man of God,' on such lips, insults the true God in the person of his prophet. Some place the hill above Samaria, others identify it with Carmel (compare 1 Kings 18:42; 2 Kings 2:25), the old site of Elijah's triumph.

The reformed Gill and the wesleyan-arminian Clarke agree the fifty came not in honour but to seize the prophet by force; it is impossible, Clarke says, that a king in Ahaziah's temper meant anything friendly.

The fullest reading is the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry's. He marvels that neither sickness nor the near approach of death softened the king: no external alarms will startle secure sinners, but rather exasperate them, so that instead of heeding the warning Ahaziah rages against the prophet who brought it. Henry catches the captain's incoherence — did he think Elijah a true prophet? then why dare to arrest him? did he think him a common man? then why send fifty to fetch him? — and he sees in the whole armed errand a shadow of the band that would come out against a greater Prophet in the garden. Elijah, once hidden in a cave, now sits openly on the hilltop, for experience of God's protection has made him bold; and when he calls down fire it is, Henry insists, neither to save nor to avenge himself but to prove his mission and to reveal the wrath of God from heaven against ungodliness.

Henry alone raises the hard question the scene forces, and answers it by an appeal to the New Testament. When the disciples later wished to call down fire on a Samaritan village, the Lord rebuked them — 'ye know not what manner of spirit ye are of' (Luke 9:54) — and Henry presses the difference: Elijah acted by a divine impulse under a dispensation of terror, in zeal for God's glory; the disciples spoke from passion, for their own reputation, and God 'judges men's practices by their principles.' The captain came trading on the word of a king; he learns, at the cost of his fifty, that the God of Israel is greater than the king of Israel, and will not be commanded down a hill.
10:
On the fire itself the witnesses concentrate their whole moral attention, and the chief question is whether Elijah's words were a curse or a prophecy. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke mounts the fullest defence. It was no more in Elijah's power to bring fire from heaven than in the captain's, he argues; God alone could send it, and God, being just and good, would not have destroyed these men had there not been sufficient cause. The fire fell 'not to please Elijah, or to gratify any vindictive humour in him, but to show his own power and justice.' Above all Clarke reads the grammar as declarative, not imprecatory: 'If I be a man of God' means 'as surely as I am a man of God, fire shall come down' — a statement of what God had already determined, not a prayer that he would. Elijah, personally, 'had no concern in the business'; he only announced it.

The grammatical-historical commentators keep to the text and its theology. Ellicott observes that the exact phrase 'let fire come down from heaven' occurs only here and at the dedication of Solomon's temple (2 Chronicles 7:1), and reads the miracle as Jehovah vindicating his own cause by the means the moment required, as once before on Carmel (1 Kings 18:38). Keil and Delitzsch note only that the second captain, undeterred by the first man's fate, will prove yet more insolent.

The reformed Gill takes the captain's mocking 'man of God' and turns it: he called Elijah so 'jeeringly,' and shall now know by the token that the title is true.

Between the schools there is no real quarrel, only a division of emphasis: Clarke labours to acquit the prophet of private vengeance and to lodge the act wholly in God's justice, while the grammatical-historical writers place it in the line of Elijah's Carmel-fire as God's public self-vindication. All agree on the point that governs the scene. The same heaven that once sent fire to consume a sacrifice and win a nation now sends it to consume the men who came to arrest its prophet, and the lesson of both is identical — that the God who answers by fire is not mocked, and that the word of his servant, spoken in his name, is as sure as the flame that seals it.
11:
The second embassy is a study in unlearning nothing, and the witnesses measure the king's hardness by his captain's haste. The grammatical-historical Ellicott marks that Ahaziah sent again 'although he had heard what had befallen his former envoys,' and quotes a Lapide on the new officer: he was 'more impudent than the first, both because he was not brought to his senses by hearing of his punishment, and because he increased his impudence by adding, Make haste.' Where the first captain said only 'Come down,' this one says 'Come down quickly' — a single word that lays bare a heart untouched by the smoking ruin at the foot of the hill.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley reads the same escalation: the second captain 'discovers more petulancy than the former, and shows how little he was moved by the former example.' The reformed Gill draws the line back to the throne — the king, 'not being at all terrified with the awful judgment upon the former, sends another' — so that the captain's insolence is only the overflow of his master's obduracy.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry sees a man 'resolved,' as it were, not to be baffled by Omnipotence itself, and warns where such obstinacy must end: sinners of this temper must at last be 'convinced and conquered by the fire of hell, for fire from heaven, it seems, will not subdue them.'

The witnesses do not debate; they diagnose. What the fire should have taught, the second summons ignores, and the divines see in that ignoring the very definition of a hardened sinner: the judgments of God, meant to warn, only provoke him to sin faster. Ahaziah spends a second fifty to prove that a king who will not bow before God can still command men to their deaths for defying him. The added word 'quickly' is the whole tragedy in miniature — a man in a hurry to repeat the mistake that has just killed fifty of his soldiers, learning speed where he should have learned fear. The divines mark, too, that the king's guilt is not lessened but doubled by the plainness of the warning he has just refused to read, and that a second fifty must die to teach him what the first had already spelled out in flame.
12:
The fire falls a second time, and the witnesses use the repetition to state plainly what is really at stake in the killing. The grammatical-historical commentators frame it. Ellicott, following Thenius, says the twice-told destruction of the captains and their companies drives home two things at once: the authority that properly belongs to the prophet, and the help and protection God bestows on his prophets. On this reading the fifties are conceived simply as instruments of a will that has set itself against Jehovah, and are dealt with accordingly (compare the mocking youths of 2 Kings 2:23 and the fiery horses of 2 Kings 6:17); so framed, Ellicott thinks, the anxious questions about the comparative guilt of individual soldiers fall away. Keil and Delitzsch note the small textual point that 'the fire of God' — lightning, in the idiom of Job — came down, and turn their eyes already to the third captain, who will prove 'better than his king, and wiser than his two predecessors.'

The reformed Gill records the plain fact: Elijah answered as before, made the same appeal, and the fire came and consumed this captain and his fifty also.

The witnesses do not quarrel over the event, though they feel its severity and do not hide it. What they will not do is sentimentalise it. The two fifties are not casual bystanders but the armed edge of a king's war on the word of God; they come, twice, to lay hands on the Lord's messenger, and twice the Lord answers for him out of heaven. The point, the divines agree, is not the arithmetic of who deserved what, but the declaration written in fire across a Samarian hillside — that the God whom Ahaziah counted powerless to tell him his fortune is powerful enough to defend his prophet, and that the man who sends soldiers against Heaven's word will find Heaven a match for his soldiers. Both fifties, the divines note, perished not as private men but as the drawn sword of a throne at war with heaven, and it is that war, not their several deserts, which the fire answers. The severity is plain, and the witnesses neither soften it nor apologise for it; they let it stand as the measure of how dangerous a thing it is to be the instrument of a king's defiance of God.
13:
With the third captain the scene breaks open into mercy, and the witnesses turn from judgment to the posture that averts it. The grammatical-historical Ellicott attends to the language of submission: the captain 'fell' on his knees — the verb used of Bel that 'boweth down' (Isaiah 46:1) — and 'besought' the prophet, the word for begging grace or favour (compare Genesis 42:21); his plea that his life and the lives of 'these fifty thy servants' be 'precious' in Elijah's sight lays deliberate stress on the number of men whose fate now hangs on a word (compare Psalms 72:14; 1 Samuel 26:21).

The reformed Gill marks how far the king's hardness had driven things — it showed him 'dreadfully hardened' to send a third fifty after two had perished — and notes the captain's telling change of approach: instead of calling up from the foot of the hill as the others had, he climbed to the top and knelt. Where the first two spoke as the king's officers, laying claim to the king's authority, this one comes up as a suppliant, pleading not his commission but the lives of his men.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley reads the kneeling rightly, as 'expressing both reverence to his person and a dread of God's judgments,' and draws the lesson the whole episode has been building toward: 'there is nothing to be got by contending with God; if we would prevail with him, it must be by supplication.' Those are wise, he adds, 'who learn submission from the fatal consequences of the obstinacy of others.'

The witnesses are of one mind. The third captain is no braver and no better placed than the two who burned; what differs is that he has read the smoking hillside as a sermon and believed it. Where his predecessors came commanding, he comes kneeling; where they invoked the king's authority, he appeals to the prophet's mercy and to the God behind him. The same errand that twice ended in fire ends this time in life, and the hinge is nothing but a bent knee and an honest dread. The chapter that has shown how surely God resists the proud now shows, in a soldier's plea, how readily he may be entreated by the humble.
14:
The captain grounds his plea in the plain evidence of the two pyres, and the witnesses read his confession as the right response to judgment and the doorway to mercy. The reformed Gill notes that he 'owns the facts and ascribes the death of them to the true cause' — the fire that came down from heaven — and comes before Elijah with 'an awful sense of the judgment of God,' fearing the same for himself and his men. The grammatical-historical commentators add the precise force of his words: Ellicott renders it 'and now, this time, let my life be precious,' the emphatic 'now' marking him off from the two captains who perished; Keil and Delitzsch carry the account forward, for it is upon this plea that the angel bids Elijah go down, and the prophet at last descends to the king.

The fullest pastoral reading is the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry's. This captain, he says, 'took warning by the fate of his predecessors,' and instead of summoning the prophet down, 'fell down before him,' begging for his life and his soldiers' with 'Let my life be precious in thy sight.' From it Henry draws the gospel of the passage: God 'is not so severe with those that stand it out against him but he is as ready to show mercy to those that repent and submit to him; never any found it in vain to cast themselves upon the mercy of God.' The courage of faith, he adds, has often struck terror into the proudest, and here submission does what defiance could not.

The witnesses do not divide. They see in the captain's speech the whole logic of the chapter turned inside out: the two who treated the man of God as a thing to be fetched met the fire that answers pride, while the one who treats him as God's servant and himself as a suppliant is spared. His honesty about the judgment — naming it, dreading it, pleading against it — is precisely what opens the way of escape; and the God who would not be mocked by a king proves, to a kneeling soldier, that he will not be outdone in mercy.
15:
The angel that opened the chapter now closes the confrontation, and the witnesses read Elijah's descent as the obedience of a man who fears God and therefore fears no one else. The grammatical-historical commentators fix the sense. Ellicott explains 'go down' as down from the mountain-top into the city, and 'be not afraid of him' as 'be not afraid of the captain' — for the two former officers, willing tools of the king, might have shown their zeal by cutting down the prophet on the spot, much as later knights would murder an archbishop at his altar. Keil and Delitzsch record simply that Elijah, so directed, followed the man to the king and repeated the word he had already sent by the messengers.

The wesleyan-arminian Clarke makes the verse a proof of Elijah's inspiration: the prophet 'had neither will nor design of his own,' but waited to know the counsel and obey the command of his God; and once bidden, 'he did not even regard his personal safety or his life,' but went straight to a king he had every reason to think doubly enraged by his prophecy and by the death of a hundred men. He goes, Clarke says, as 'the ambassador of the King eternal,' his honour and his life alike in his Master's hands.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry adds the note of dignity: Elijah had refused the earlier summonses not from fear of the court but because he would not be 'imperiously compelled,' which would have lessened his Master's honour; now, bidden by God, he goes down boldly and 'magnifies his office.'

The witnesses agree that the same fearlessness governs both the refusing and the going. Elijah would not be dragged down a hill by an armed captain trading on a king's name; he walks down it freely at a word from the Angel of the Lord. The difference is the whole difference between a prophet and a courtier: he moves not at the pleasure of the throne but at the command of heaven, and so he can stand, unarmed and unafraid, in the bedchamber of the man he has just condemned to die.
16:
Face to face with the king, Elijah speaks the sentence a third time without softening a syllable, and the witnesses gather around its boldness and its bare repetition. The wesleyan-arminian Wesley catches the drama: the prophet delivers the doom 'to his very face,' and yet 'the king durst not lay hands upon him,' being 'daunted with the prophet's presence and confidence, and affrighted by the late dreadful evidence of his power with God.' The condemned messenger walks out of the lion's den untouched.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the form of the speech. Ellicott notes that the accusing question is parenthetic — 'forasmuch as thou hast sent to inquire of Baal-zebub . . . therefore thou shalt not come down' — and that the words of the oracle are now 'thrice repeated verbally,' to the messengers, by the messengers, and to the king in person. He quotes Baehr's remark that Elijah, here as elsewhere, 'reappears suddenly and disappears again, and no one knows whence he comes or whither he goes,' and infers that the story's peculiar shape bespeaks an origin in living oral tradition rather than a written source. Keil and Delitzsch simply register that the oracle is repeated once more, verbatim.

The reformed Gill notes that Elijah, admitted to the chamber and after some discourse, confirmed to the king's face exactly what he had first sent by his servants — the charge unchanged, the sentence unchanged.

The witnesses do not divide. What impresses them is that the word does not grow milder as it nears the throne. To the couriers on the road it was 'thou shalt surely die'; from the couriers to the king it was the same; and now, in the sickroom, with the power of life and death seemingly in the king's hand, it is the same again. The prophet mitigates nothing for fear of the royal displeasure or in pity for the royal misery. The God of Israel, whom Ahaziah had passed over as though he were not there, has the last word in the king's own chamber — and it is the word the king refused to seek.
17:
The verse records the word's fulfilment and then opens a famous chronological knot, and the witnesses split along predictable lines over how to untie it. On the fulfilment they are one: the reformed Gill notes that Ahaziah 'died of the sickness, and on the bed to which he went up, as he said,' the event answering the oracle to the letter, and — since the king died childless — his brother Jehoram took the throne.

The knot is the date: 'in the second year of Jehoram the son of Jehoshaphat, king of Judah.' It clashes with 2 Kings 3:1, which dates Joram of Israel's accession to Jehoshaphat's eighteenth year, and with 1 Kings 22:51 and 2 Kings 8:16 besides. The wesleyan-arminian commentators reach for co-regency. Clarke lays out Calmet's solution and then Lightfoot's fuller one: while Jehoshaphat and Ahab were away at the war for Ramoth-gilead, each made his son viceroy in his stead, so that Jehoram of Judah's 'reign' can be counted from that earlier vice-royalty; reckon from it, and Jehoshaphat's eighteenth year is indeed the 'second year of Jehoram his son.' Wesley gives the same reconciliation in brief, dating the viceroyalty to Jehoshaphat's seventeenth year and threading the several regnal notices through it (compare 1 Kings 22:42; 1 Kings 16:29).

The grammatical-historical Ellicott is readier to suspect the text. He notes that the Septuagint, Syriac, and Vulgate add 'his brother' — a phrase fallen out of the Hebrew — and that the Vatican Greek reads 'in the eighteenth year,' which he thinks probably original; either the present Hebrew is corrupt, or the compiler here followed a different source, and Thenius would emend the figure outright. Keil and Delitzsch likewise register the discrepancy without forcing it.

The debate is genuine but courteous. One party harmonises by history — overlapping co-regencies that make two 'first years' possible for the same man — while the other harmonises by text-criticism, preferring the Greek 'eighteenth year' or positing a compiler's seam. Neither doubts the substance the verse exists to record: that the word of the Lord spoken by Elijah stood, that Ahaziah died on schedule and heirless, and that the crown passed, exactly as the God of Israel had determined, out of the hand that had reached for the god of Ekron.
18:
The chapter ends on the historian's standing formula, and the witnesses, few and agreed, read it as both a full stop and a signpost. The grammatical-historical Ellicott glosses the terms: 'the acts' is the Hebrew dibre, the 'history' or record of his doings, and he notes the manuscript variant 'and all that he did'; the 'book of the chronicles of the kings of Israel' is the same lost royal annal cited throughout these histories (compare 1 Kings 14:19), a source the inspired writer draws upon and points his readers to, but which has not survived. Keil and Delitzsch simply let the closing sentence stand as the seal on the reign.

The reformed Gill supplies the poignant measure of the thing: Ahaziah's acts 'must be but few,' for his 'two years' reign' was itself imperfect and cut short. Two years — set against his father Ahab's twenty-two — is all the record this king leaves, and the divines feel the weight of the contrast.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry had already drawn the moral from the death that precedes this notice: some sinners God lets 'live long, to aggravate their judgment,' and others he cuts down 'soon, to hasten it,' but 'evil pursues sinners, and, sooner or later, it will overtake them.'

The witnesses do not contend over so plain a verse. They use it to close the account as Scripture closes every reign — with a reference to fuller records now beyond our reach, and a silence that is itself eloquent. What the chapter has shown of Ahaziah is nearly all we shall ever know of him: a broken lattice, a message to Ekron, a prophet on a hill, fire from heaven, and a sentence that held. The rest is filed away in a book no one can now open, and the divines are content to let it lie there, remarking only that a life which sought the god of flies and spurned the God of Israel had little in it worth the preserving, and that the brevity of the record is the last of the judgments the chapter has to report.
